article.title: Medicare's ACCESS Model Gives AI Chronic Care Agents Their First Federal Payment Mechanism
article.summary: CMS's 10-year ACCESS program—launching July 5 with 150 digital health participants—creates the first federal reimbursement pathway designed to pay for AI agents that monitor and coordinate care for patients between clinic visits.
article.body_markdown:
## Overview

For the first time in the history of American healthcare, the federal government is creating a formal payment mechanism for AI agents that operate outside the clinic walls. The Centers for Medicare & Medicaid Services will launch the ACCESS model—Advancing Chronic Care with Effective, Scalable Solutions—on July 5, 2026, enrolling more than 150 digital health companies in a 10-year voluntary program that rewards measurable patient outcomes rather than clinician time.

As [TechCrunch](https://techcrunch.com/2026/05/12/medicares-new-payment-model-is-built-for-ai-and-most-of-the-tech-world-has-no-idea/) put it: "There is no governmental mechanism to pay for an AI agent that monitors a patient between visits, calls to check in, coordinates a housing referral, or makes sure someone picks up their medication. ACCESS creates that mechanism for the first time."

## What We Know

The ACCESS model was [announced by CMS](https://www.aha.org/news/headline/2026-04-14-cms-accepts-more-150-organizations-participation-access-model-extends-application-deadline) on April 13, 2026, and accepted more than 150 organizations for the inaugural cohort, with an application deadline extended to May 15. A second cohort is scheduled to begin January 1, 2027, according to [Prism News](https://www.prismnews.com/news/cms-launches-10-year-medicare-model-to-pay-ai-chronic-care). More than 350 technology-enabled care organizations submitted letters of intent to apply, reflecting the breadth of industry interest.

The program introduces what CMS calls Outcome-Aligned Payments—"a structure that rewards results rather than a checklist of required activities," as [Prism News](https://www.prismnews.com/news/cms-launches-10-year-medicare-model-to-pay-ai-chronic-care) described it. Participating organizations receive payment based on whether patients with qualifying conditions reach measurable health goals, such as reduced blood pressure or lower pain levels. The conditions covered span those affecting over two-thirds of Medicare beneficiaries: high blood pressure, diabetes, chronic musculoskeletal pain, and depression, as well as chronic kidney disease, obesity, and anxiety, according to [DistilINFO](https://distilinfo.com/2026/05/13/medicare-access-model-revolutionizes-ai-healthcare-payments/) and [Prism News](https://www.prismnews.com/news/cms-launches-10-year-medicare-model-to-pay-ai-chronic-care).

Participating companies must be enrolled in Medicare Part B, designate a licensed physician as clinical director, and demonstrate FHIR-based data exchange capability, [The Momentum notes](https://www.themomentum.ai/blog/understanding-cmss-new-access-model-a-guide-for-tech-enabled-providers). Eligible services encompass telehealth, wearable monitoring devices, and digital coaching tools.

The program was designed by Abe Sutton, director of the CMS Innovation Center and a former venture capitalist at Rubicon Founders, and Jacob Shiff, CMS's chief AI and technology officer and a former healthcare founder, according to [TechCrunch](https://techcrunch.com/2026/05/12/medicares-new-payment-model-is-built-for-ai-and-most-of-the-tech-world-has-no-idea/).

Private payers representing 165 million Americans have pledged alignment with the outcome-based payment approach, suggesting the model could reshape reimbursement structures well beyond traditional Medicare, [Prism News reported](https://www.prismnews.com/news/cms-launches-10-year-medicare-model-to-pay-ai-chronic-care). Private insurers have historically followed Medicare reimbursement changes within 18 to 24 months, according to [TechCrunch](https://techcrunch.com/2026/05/12/medicares-new-payment-model-is-built-for-ai-and-most-of-the-tech-world-has-no-idea/).

## A Case Study in Lean AI Operations

Pair Team, a care coordination company founded in 2019, is among the organizations entering the inaugural cohort. Its founder and chief executive, Neil Batlivala, has been explicit about what the new payment model demands: "The reimbursement rates have to be low. The economics only work if you're running a lean, AI-first operation," he told [TechCrunch](https://techcrunch.com/2026/05/12/medicares-new-payment-model-is-built-for-ai-and-most-of-the-tech-world-has-no-idea/).

The company's staff of approximately 850 clinical professionals is supported by a voice AI agent called Flora, deployed roughly nine months ago, that handles patient intake, referrals, and check-ins around the clock, according to [TechCrunch](https://techcrunch.com/2026/05/12/medicares-new-payment-model-is-built-for-ai-and-most-of-the-tech-world-has-no-idea/). Pair Team has raised approximately $30 million from investors including Kleiner Perkins, Kraft Ventures, and Next Ventures, and reports nine-figure annual revenue. It currently reaches approximately 500,000 potential patients and is targeting 1 million within three years.

The company's approach has clinical support: a peer-reviewed study published in the Journal of General Internal Medicine found that Pair Team's community-integrated model resulted in one in four hospital visits and one in two emergency room visits being prevented among Medicaid members living with homelessness, serious mental illness, and chronic disease, [TechCrunch reported](https://techcrunch.com/2026/05/12/medicares-new-payment-model-is-built-for-ai-and-most-of-the-tech-world-has-no-idea/).

## What We Don't Know

Whether the ACCESS model will achieve meaningful cost savings remains genuinely uncertain. A 2023 Congressional Budget Office analysis found that the CMS Innovation Center increased federal spending by $5.4 billion during its first decade rather than producing the savings it projected, as [TechCrunch noted](https://techcrunch.com/2026/05/12/medicares-new-payment-model-is-built-for-ai-and-most-of-the-tech-world-has-no-idea/). The program's deliberately low reimbursement rates have already disappointed some prospective participants who had anticipated higher payments.

The program covers Medicare's 67 million beneficiaries, according to [TechCrunch](https://techcrunch.com/2026/05/12/medicares-new-payment-model-is-built-for-ai-and-most-of-the-tech-world-has-no-idea/), though the practical reach of the first cohort will depend on how quickly participants can scale enrollment. The full participant list has not been publicly disclosed, and CMS has not yet specified how it will audit outcome claims or verify AI agent interventions.

Healthcare AI funding reached $6.8 billion in 2025, according to [TechCrunch](https://techcrunch.com/2026/05/12/medicares-new-payment-model-is-built-for-ai-and-most-of-the-tech-world-has-no-idea/), suggesting investor appetite for this category is already substantial. ACCESS gives that capital a reimbursement pathway it has previously lacked.

## Analysis

The structural significance of ACCESS lies not in any single participating company but in what it legitimizes: a care model where the work between appointments—the calls, check-ins, coordination, and reminders that fall outside the exam room—is formally reimbursable for the first time. Traditional fee-for-service Medicare has always paid for clinician time; ACCESS attempts to pay for what that time cannot cover.

For AI companies building in chronic care, the model is less an invitation than a test. Payment rates are structured to be low by design, making automation a financial necessity rather than an efficiency choice. Organizations that cannot operate at scale with lean AI-first workflows will find the economics prohibitive. Those that can will have the first federally legitimized revenue model for AI agents operating continuously between patient visits.
